attributes.stem:
A 56-year-old woman is referred to the hospital nephrology service for investigation of proteinuria and microscopic haematuria. She has developed progressive renal impairment over the past two months, with a current estimated glomerular filtration rate of 25 mL/min/1.73m2 (>90). She reports recurrent sinusitis over the past year and gradual onset of hearing loss. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 178/98 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. She has a saddle nose deformity. A renal biopsy has been arranged.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following laboratory tests is most likely to be positive?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suggestive of granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), including renal involvement (proteinuria, haematuria, renal impairment) and characteristic upper respiratory tract manifestations (saddle nose deformity, sinusitis, hearing loss). The combination of these features makes PR3-ANCA the most likely positive test, as it is present in approximately 70-90% of patients with active, generalised GPA. GPA is a type of anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic autoantibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis (AAV).

Anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies are associated with Goodpasture's syndrome, which typically presents with pulmonary haemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Whilst diagnostic confirmation with a renal biopsy is required, positive PR3-ANCA fits more with the clinical scenario.

MPO-ANCA is more commonly associated with microscopic polyangiitis and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA). While it can be positive in some cases of GPA, PR3-ANCA is much more specific for GPA, particularly in the presence of upper respiratory tract involvement.

Anti-double stranded DNA antibodies are associated with systemic lupus erythematosus. While lupus nephritis can cause similar renal manifestations, the upper respiratory tract involvement would be unusual, and other features of SLE would typically be present.

Anti-phospholipase A2 receptor antibodies are specific for primary membranous nephropathy. While this can cause proteinuria, it would not explain the systemic manifestations, particularly the upper respiratory tract involvement.

GPA is a systemic vasculitis that primarily affects small vessels. The classic triad includes upper respiratory tract disease, lung involvement, and glomerulonephritis. Early recognition and treatment with immunosuppression is crucial to prevent organ damage.
learningPoints: PR3-ANCA is strongly associated with granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), particularly in patients with upper respiratory tract involvement.
The classic triad of GPA includes upper respiratory tract disease, lung involvement, and glomerulonephritis.
Saddle nose deformity is a characteristic feature of GPA due to cartilage destruction in the nasal septum.
GPA is a type of anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic autoantibody (ANCA)-associated vasculitis (AAV).
references: Rout P, Qurie A. Granulomatosis With Polyangiitis. In: StatPearls 2024 [online]. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/books/NBK557827/?report=reader
